utterances: The New York City Council and the state assembly are currently drafting bills that would create more workplace regulations in nail salons.
Lawmakers are considering modeling part of the legislation on a California statute that requires cosmetic companies to disclose if any of their ingredients are potentially harmful.
But in most places, it will be up to workers to try and educate themselves.
For NPR News, I'm Kathleen Horan in New York.
Retailers report cheerful news this Christmas shopping season.
Sales are up over last year, but way, way up in high-end stores, like Tiffany's; while Wal-Mart reports brisk sales on baby formula on the days when unemployment checks arrive.
We want to hear from retailers.
What's moving?
What isn't?
Are shoppers buying Coach bags or the knockoffs this holiday season?
Email us, talk@npr.org.
You can also join the conversation on our website.
That's at npr.org.
Click on TALK OF THE NATION.
Ylan Mui is a retail reporter for The Washington Post and wrote a story on this polarized Christmas season.
She joins us by phone from her office.
Nice to have you back on the program.
Oh, thank you for having me.
And give us a bit more details.
It's not just Tiffany's doing well but the high-end items at Tiffany's.
Right.

What we're finding is that you're seeing a sort of divide in how Americans are recovering in this post-recessionary environment, and we're finding that high-end retailers or luxury retailers are doing quite well this holiday season.
In fact, so far, sales at luxury stores are up about 7 percent compared to last year.
So they all got in the car and went to church to see Dr. King preach.
After the service, Mavis recalls, Pops Staples said this to his daughters...
(SOUNDBITE OF PANEL DISCUSSION)
…of the stream is the family relationships.
And it means that when immigrants come to this country, they have a base of support, either family or job.
They have an established set of relationships that allow them to integrate successfully and become fully American.
And when you look around the world, we are one of the outstanding success stories in our legal immigration system.
And what this point system would do would be to throw out those relationships and say they don't matter anymore.

And instead, you have this anonymous set of points that you can qualify for or not, and some kind of hierarchy that, of course, is going to reward high skill, high-tech, highly educated and to the detriment of family relationships, and disconnected from an actual employment relationship.
And I think that's a radical social experiment.
I think it certainly has some attractions and might be something we should look at in an experimental way.
Let's take a certain number of the visas that we allocate each year and design this point system and try it out in a pilot way.
And let's see if it meets our needs.
Let's see how it works.
But let's not dismantle what we have already that's been working well at the cost of putting this brand new radical proposal in place.
Well, also here with us in studio 3A is Tamar Jacoby.
She's a senior fellow at The Manhattan Institute.
It's nice to have you back on TALK OF THE NATION.
Good to be here.
And I know you have a different view.
I do.
I think the critics are exaggerating - for one thing - how much change there will be.
Fifty percent, at least, something like 50 percent of the visas will still go to family members and employer-employee ties will still play a role in who gets points.

But I think in a world where arguably - millions, and arguably billions of people want to come to the United States and we're struggling to be competitive with other countries in attracting immigrants.
And we can only let in a limited number.
It makes sense to ask ourselves who do we want and why.
I don't think the answer should be only people with advance degrees, who have a credential in another country.
I think we do want to reward hard work and enterprise and - even at the low end of the economic scale because we need those kinds of workers too.

But I think to say let's have a system where we think hard about who we want and assign points, and make those kinds of judgments, I think that makes all the sense in the world in a globalizing competitive world.
Well, I think we've got lessons we could take and learn by looking at some other Western industrialized countries that have implemented solely a point system.
And when you have a situation, such as in Canada, where you've got Ph.D.s driving taxicabs, I think there may be something amiss.

I don't disagree with Tamar that if you assign points properly and allocate properly, maybe you could design something that would continue to respect and help families stay anchored and connected to their immediate loved ones, maybe you could award points for an existing employer relationship.
I just want to challenge that again…
But…
…on immediate loved ones.
Spouses and children would still get priority.
You can still bring them in.
Well, no. No. Spouses and minor children of citizens…
Right.
…still can come in as now in unlimited numbers.
But in this scheme in the Senate bill, spouses and minor children of lawful permanent residents would be numerically capped at 87,000, which is our current rate.
And which has led to five-year, seven-year backlogs.
So that's not working.
We need more numbers overall in some of these categories.
But the people who are…
And in the system…
Tamar Jacoby?
They say that adult siblings have an absolute right to come and should come uncapped, I think, which is what the current system does.
No.
I think that…
That's not uncapped.
Absolutely not.
Well, but they have -
It's capped at 65,000.
It's capped.
It's capped.
But they have a right to come, ultimately.
They have to wait a long time.
Should they live so long.
(Soundbite of laughter)
They have a right to come.
I think adult siblings, arguably adult married children - I think we - there maybe other people we value more, and that supply is short.
I think we need more green cards.
One of the things I like to see the bill do is…
I think you both agree on that.
I think we agree on that.
Let's see if we can get somebody else in on this.
Caroline is with us.
Caroline calling from Davis, California.
Hi.
Hi.
…expand the number.

But I think if we're given that there's a short supply and that we have to allocate them, I think you do want to - I think to say that there's a limit to how much a role family should play is reasonable.

But the place I'd like to see the system - I want to make - I'd like to see the system that they're proposing tweaked a little bit, is I do think it's important that a Mexican who comes as a dishwasher and makes it up to hotel manager, I think he's just as valuable to us as the Bosnian engineer, who's never been to America.
And I think it's very important that the point system be able to accommodate him as well.
And it's not totally clear yet whether it's going to.
It does have some - it does allocate some points to working hard and being in a high-demand occupation.
But to me, that's the most important thing here.
Let's recognize merit, but not just credential merit, also merit that's about how you behave here.
